videos.description:
Oi genteee 💖 Blog das irmãs faz MELHOR COMIDA DO APLICATIVO PRO CHERU GANHA. Hoje a Jeru e a Maru vão tentar escolher a melhor comida que tem no aplicativo para o Cheru!

Se você gostou desse vídeo, clique no JOINHA pra gente, deixe um COMENTÁRIO abaixo dizendo o que você achou, COMPARTILHE o vídeo com os seus amigos e se INSCREVA no canal se você ainda não é inscrito 😍
-------------------------------------------------------------
💌 PARA PARCERIAS: blogdasirmas@redezappiens.com.br
📬 CAIXA POSTAL: Nº 58 CEP 19700.970 Paraguaçu Paulista - SP

videos.description:
Oi genteee 💖 Blog das irmãs faz MELHOR COMIDA DO APLICATIVO PRO LUCAS GANHA. Maru e Jeru vão escolher uma comida para o Lucas, e ele vai ter que decidir qual comida foi a vencedora!
-------------------------------------------------------------
Se você gostou desse vídeo, clique no JOINHA pra gente, deixe um COMENTÁRIO abaixo dizendo o que você achou, COMPARTILHE o vídeo com os seus amigos e se INSCREVA no canal se você ainda não é inscrito 😍
-------------------------------------------------------------
🥰NOSSOS OUTROS CANAIS🥰
MARU E BOMBA
JERU E CHERU

videos.title: FLUMINENSE 3 X 1 CORINTHIANS | MELHORES MOMENTOS | CAMPEONATO BRASILEIRO 2026
videos.description:
NOITE QUENTE NO MARACANÃ! 

O #Fluminense venceu o #Corinthians por 3 a 1 pela 9ª rodada do #CampeonatoBrasileiro. Em uma noite muito quente no Maracanã, os comandados de #Zubeldia foram muito superiores ao time de #DorivalJr, conquistaram três pontos e seguem na briga pelo topo da tabela da competição. 

Na primeira etapa, o Fluzão abriu o placar com #JohnKennedy e ampliou com #Hercules. Com jogadas bem trabalhadas, o Time de Guerreiros dominou o jogo e fez o goleiro #Fábio trabalhar muito pouco. 

Já nos 45 finais, os donos da casa seguiram com total controle da partida e o argentino #Castillo saiu do banco e fez o terceiro. O Alvinegro do Parque São Jorge diminuiu para os visitantes, mas não foi o suficiente para esboçar uma reação no Rio de Janeiro. 

Campeonato Brasileiro tá no Modo Prime e toda rodada é um jogo exclusivo aqui no #PrimeVideo! No domingo (5), temos Corinthians x Internacional. Esporte ao vivo, o ano todo, TÁ NO AMAZON PRIME!

videos.title: FLUMINENSE 3 X 1 CORINTHIANS | MELHORES MOMENTOS | CAMPEONATO BRASILEIRO 2026
videos.description:
NOITE QUENTE NO MARACANÃ! 

O #Fluminense venceu o #Corinthians por 3 a 1 pela 9ª rodada do #CampeonatoBrasileiro. Em uma noite muito quente no Maracanã, os comandados de #Zubeldia foram muito superiores ao time de #DorivalJr, conquistaram três pontos e seguem na briga pelo topo da tabela da competição. 

Na primeira etapa, o Fluzão abriu o placar com #JohnKennedy e ampliou com #Hercules. Com jogadas bem trabalhadas, o Time de Guerreiros dominou o jogo e fez o goleiro #Fábio trabalhar muito pouco. 

Já nos 45 finais, os donos da casa seguiram com total controle da partida e o argentino #Castillo saiu do banco e fez o terceiro. O Alvinegro do Parque São Jorge diminuiu para os visitantes, mas não foi o suficiente para esboçar uma reação no Rio de Janeiro. 

Campeonato Brasileiro tá no Modo Prime e toda rodada é um jogo exclusivo aqui no #PrimeVideo! No domingo (5), temos Corinthians x Internacional. Esporte ao vivo, o ano todo, TÁ NO AMAZON PRIME!

⚽ Placar final: Fluminense 3 x 1 Corinthians

videos.title: FLUMINENSE 3 X 1 CORINTHIANS | MELHORES MOMENTOS | CAMPEONATO BRASILEIRO 2026
videos.description:
NOITE QUENTE NO MARACANÃ! 

O #Fluminense venceu o #Corinthians por 3 a 1 pela 9ª rodada do #CampeonatoBrasileiro. Em uma noite muito quente no Maracanã, os comandados de #Zubeldia foram muito superiores ao time de #DorivalJr, conquistaram três pontos e seguem na briga pelo topo da tabela da competição. 

Na primeira etapa, o Fluzão abriu o placar com #JohnKennedy e ampliou com #Hercules. Com jogadas bem trabalhadas, o Time de Guerreiros dominou o jogo e fez o goleiro #Fábio trabalhar muito pouco. 

Já nos 45 finais, os donos da casa seguiram com total controle da partida e o argentino #Castillo saiu do banco e fez o terceiro. O Alvinegro do Parque São Jorge diminuiu para os visitantes, mas não foi o suficiente para esboçar uma reação no Rio de Janeiro. 

Campeonato Brasileiro tá no Modo Prime e toda rodada é um jogo exclusivo aqui no #PrimeVideo! No domingo (5), temos Corinthians x Internacional. Esporte ao vivo, o ano todo, TÁ NO AMAZON PRIME!

Tem ali da economia, da educação, da saúde, eh tem ali, quem faz a interlocução com o Congresso Nacional, mas quem é responsável por todas as nomeações e exonerações é o da Casa Civil. No governo Bolsonaro era o bandidão Ciro Nogueira. No governo Lula é o Rui Costa. Se você não sabe também, Rui Costa é do PT da Bahia, de onde é também o Jack Wagner. videos.title: A surpreendente estratégia de guerra do Irã - Robinson Farinazzo - Programa 20 Minutos
videos.description:
A surpreendente estratégia de guerra do Irã - Robinson Farinazzo - Programa 20 Minutos

🌍 Neste episódio urgente do Programa 20 Minutos, dissecamos a mais nova e ousada jogada do tabuleiro geopolítico global: o fechamento do Estreito de Hormuz pelo Irã como resposta direta à guerra declarada por Donald Trump contra a nação persa em fevereiro de 2026. Enquanto o mundo assistia a uma possível escalada convencional, o Irã surpreendeu ao acionar sua arma mais letal — o gargalo por onde escoa cerca de 20% do petróleo mundial — transformando um ponto estratégico em instrumento de asfixia econômica global. Com a análise contundente de Robinson Farinazzo, vamos entender como essa estratégia de guerra assimétrica não apenas neutralizou a superioridade militar americana na região, mas também expôs a fragilidade da cadeia de suprimentos ocidental, elevando o preço do barril e forçando potências mundiais a repensarem suas alianças. Descubra os bastidores dessa decisão, os riscos de uma conflagração maior e como o Irã, ao fechar a torneira do Golfo Pérsico, redefiniu as regras do conflito e desafiou a hegemonia dos Estados Unidos no Oriente Médio de forma avassaladora. Esta é uma aula de geopolítica que você não pode perder.

🚀 ASSISTA AGORA e entenda como o Irã travou o mundo com uma única manobra!
📌 INSCREVA-SE no canal e ative o sino para acompanhar todas as atualizações sobre o conflito!

videos.title: "Catástrofe total": Coronel Macgregor critica duramente Trump em reação impulsiva ao discurso sob...
videos.description:
O Coronel Douglas Macgregor (aposentado) é um consultor de risco geopolítico com décadas de experiência em defesa e política externa dos EUA.

Nesta edição do ANI Broadcast com Ishaan Prakash, o Coronel Macgregor apresenta sua primeira reação ao discurso de Trump sobre o Irã, chamando-o de "teatro do absurdo" e alertando para uma catástrofe iminente. Ele afirma que Trump "perdeu o rumo" e que a guerra vai piorar.

#ANIBroadcast #IshaanPrakash #Macgregor #DonaldTrump #Irã #Hormuz #PetróleoBruto #Netanyahu #Israel #Golfo #IdadeDaPedra

videos.title: Guerra do Irã AO VIVO | “RISCO DE GUERRA NUCLEAR É REAL” | Trump intensifica ataque | Coronel Mac...
videos.description:
#Irã #Trump #Israel

Barkha Dutt entra AO VIVO com o renomado analista militar Douglas Macgregor enquanto Donald Trump se dirige à nação, prometendo intensificar os ataques dos EUA contra o Irã nas próximas 2 a 3 semanas para "terminar o trabalho". O anúncio fez com que os preços globais do petróleo disparassem, decepcionando os mercados que esperavam uma redução da escalada na guerra com o Irã.

Macgregor classifica o conflito como "insano" e afirma que o discurso de Trump ficou muito aquém das expectativas. Ele argumenta que o envolvimento dos EUA está intimamente ligado aos objetivos estratégicos de Israel, alertando que o risco de uma guerra regional mais ampla está crescendo, incluindo o perigo real de uma escalada nuclear.

Em uma forte mensagem para a Índia, Macgregor insta Narendra Modi a intervir diplomaticamente e dialogar com Trump, alertando que as consequências econômicas, especialmente o aumento dos preços dos combustíveis, podem em breve afetar as famílias indianas.

A conversa também examina a possibilidade de uma operação terrestre limitada dos EUA no Irã, destacando os sérios riscos que ela representa para as tropas americanas, a estabilidade do Oriente Médio e a economia global.
videos.transcript: Pesquisar transcrição A very very good morning to all of our viewers in India and across the world. It's a little short of 7:30 in the morning here in India and of course uh getting to be about 10:00 at night in the United States of America where the American President Donald Trump has moments ago finished his address to the nation. Now, for those who are anticipating that he would either announce the exit of the United States from a war that has created cataclysmic and catastrophic consequences around the world, well, that exit announcement, the end of the war did not happen. for those who were looking to Trump's address to the nation to indicate whether the next step would be some sort of ground invasion, limited ground operation, commando action. Well, clearly the American president didn't spell that out and perhaps he couldn't have been expected to either. But what he did indicate is that American troops would be moving ahead to quote unquote finish the job. He said that this was because this was the only way to honor American troops who had been placed in harm's way in this war. It is not the news that the markets, the global economy, and much of the world would have wanted to hear. He did set a time frame, however, the same time frame that we've been hearing for his from his administration for the last many days, two to three weeks. He said most goals, most strategic objectives of the United States of America had been achieved and very shortly and he put a number on it two to three weeks the task would be completely finished. He said talks the negotiations that he had earlier claimed were going really well would continue but if there was no deal then Iran was going to be hit very very hard. The bottom line we simply do not know where we stand. All we know is that there is no end yet to this war. In a moment from now, we'll be going live to the United States of America to talk to a top global military analyst, a geopolitical risk analyst, somebody who's also been a former adviser to the US Secretary of Defense. We will be joined by Colonel Douglas McGregor in a couple of minutes. I'm Barcadat, you're with the Mojo story. On the program today on this special live broadcast, we are going to be decoding Trump's address to the nation. As I said, for those who were expecting that he was going to announce the exit or the end of the war, that did not happen. Instead, Trump doubled down and spoke about how it's going to take at least another two to three weeks before America can achieve all of its objectives. He claimed that most of these objectives had nearly been achieved and in two to three weeks they would be absolutely finished. He used the phrase finish the job to describe what the troops were planning to do next. Ahead of the American president's address to the nation, the A10 attack fleet, the Thunderbolt, had already begun movement towards West Asia, raising anticipation and apprehensions of what Trump's planning next. Would it be some sort of ground invasion? Would it be a limited commando action? We simply do not know. In the meanwhile, it continued to be a night of uh war. Uh for its part, Iran has continued to target uh Israel. Tel Aviv came under intense and enormous bombardment. this even as Iran's president, president Khan released a letter directly addressing uh the American people to say that the American people were not the enemy of Iran. We should remind our audience that Trump's approval ratings are at an all-time low, historically low for any American president and this war is extremely unpopular for him at home. And yet that address to the nation did not bring any closure to the war. I want to introduce our newsmaker early this morning night for him in the United States of America. Colonel Douglas McGregor is with us. He's a top global military analyst. He's a geopolitical risk adviser. He's been uh an adviser formerly to the US Secretary of Defense. We appreciate your time uh Colonel. Uh it's good to see you and I just want to get straight to it because I know you're a busy man and we've got limited time with you. disappointed with the president's speech. Yes, I would say disappointment is an understatement. I had really hoped that he would take into account the really terrible impact this war is having on the global economy. You know, again, our friends around the world are being harmed. The Japanese, the Koreans, you, India is being harmed. Uh you do $180 billion dollars in trade every year with the Gulf States. Those Gulf states, I don't think, are going to survive now. They're in bad shape at this point, but I think the next two to three weeks could be devastating. And it's not just the Gulf States. I I think the whole global economy is in trouble. And I was hoping that he would change course. I really was because he's using a sledgehammer to fix a cell phone. This is a 20th century method of operation. You could have seen this happen in Korea, in Vietnam, and we did. Uh, and and I can tell you frankly, these these massive bombing attacks, even though they become more price, more precise over time, they're devastating, but they're not effective. And you know, if this has been so successful, why is it that every time he gives a speech and talks about how great everything is, you know, the Iranians launch tactical and theater ballistic missiles at all of their opponents and drones. I mean there's no evidence that the capability to reach out with this connectivity, thisworked ISR, intelligence, surveillance, reconnaissance in space and in the terrestrial environment that's also with all the strike systems all over Iran. Some people call it a mosaic strategy, but they're distributed. They're all over the country. They can converge and deliver enormous precise firepower very quickly. I don't see any evidence that that capability has been seriously affected. And they have these underground missile cities and there are there's a lot underground that's stored that hasn't been used. So I I'm I'm discouraged to hear him say those things. The other part of this is if we're so successful, what do we need ground troops for? I thought that this brilliant air naval complex was going to win without any ground troops. They weren't necessary. Of course, the initial expectation was that Iran would fall apart. 96 hours. And now we're talking about bombing Iran into the Stone Age. That's insane. Why? What interest could we possibly have in doing that to the Iranian people? And he says that's where they belong. I mean, these are ridiculous statements that no president, certainly none in my lifetime that I can remember, would have ever made ever about anybody. So, I I think it's the wrong approach. I don't think it's going to work. And I think that the use of ground forces is very dangerous because the same precision weapons, the same uh Chinese and Russian provided satellite coverage is going to be very dangerous for them. It's it's going to it's going to put anybody we put on the ground over there uh in a very difficult and dangerous position. So, I don't see how this opens the straight of Hormuse. I I just don't uh I I'll get to the straight of hormones in just a moment, but may I ask you, Colonel, uh listening to his speech and reading between the lines, is ground action or attempted ground action by American troops now inevitable? Because what else are they going to be doing for the next two or three weeks? You know, I I can't say. Uh they obviously have built up a substantial number of them. I think it's going to reach 20,000 very shortly. Uh but the question is how do you get them there? Most of the United States Navy is hundreds of miles away from the Persian Gulf. They discovered that if they were close, they were going to be struck by missiles. They would be at risk. And so, the Navy has moved away. Now, the Houthis have joined the fight. I suspect the Houthis will probably close the entrance to the Red Sea, which means now you're you're dealing with enemies on on two flanks. You're having to fight in multiple directions at the same time. We're We probably have restocked our air defense missiles, our air and missile defense missiles, but I don't know how many and I don't know how many we still have. We we've obviously used thousands of them. I say that because every time an incoming missile is directed at us, we usually fire two missiles back. Uh so I I I don't know how long we can keep up an intensive bombardment. Now, I think we we expended something like 11,000 precisiong guided missiles and munitions in the first several days at the cost of billions and billions of dollars and that didn't work. I suspect we're going to do that again. But at the same time, you pointed out in just when you came in, you talked about the A10. That is a ground support weapon. That's exclusively what it's used for. And it's very good, but it too is vulnerable. And uh I heard the president say that the Iranians have no air defense. He said they have no anti-aircraft ability. That's not true. Uh and that's not true particularly when you're dealing with ground troops because you've got to come in lower to support them effectively. So at any rate, uh I'm I'm very very disappointed and I'm afraid things are going to get a lot worse. Can we talk about the uh the ATM the Thunderbolt fleet for just a moment? uh this fleet was actually moved even before Trump addressed the nation and it led to widespread speculation in American media and across the world as you mentioned that this is a ground support weapon. What is your biggest fear right now that he's going to go in for some crazed uh what he thinks is a limited ground operation either to take the Cara island or Fox News was reporting uh that he has taken a briefing on whether it's possible to have a commando operation to move uranium. It all sounds frankly insane. And I use that word because it's a word you've used for this war. When you looked at the A10 fleet being moved, when you hear about all this news about the sort of ground ops he's considering as a top military man, what do you think? Do you go, "Oh my god, what's going through your head right now?" Well, you're putting uh small numbers of troops. And you know, I know people will say, "Well, 10,000, that's a big number." But you're not going to put 10,000 on one of these islands. So you're putting small numbers of people in a very vulnerable position. If you're light infantry, the most powerful weapon you have in your organization, frankly, is the radio because it allows you to reach aircraft and artillery. Now, I don't think we're going to get much closein artillery support from the fleet because the fleet's afraid of being sunk, which means that you're entirely dependent upon air power. Now, the A10 is lethal because it can orbit above you at what what looks like a leisurely pace. It has excellent optics. It can pick up lots of information and identify targets on the ground. And its cannon 30mm and 105 millimeter cannon are very, very accurate. If they can sit up there and orbit without being shot down, they can have a terrible impact on the enemy and they can help keep the the infantry on the ground alive, but they may not be able to. During the Kosovo air campaign, which I was involved with, we used some A-10s. And the A-10 found that whenever it dropped below 15,000 ft, all the aircraft discovered this, they were immediately taken under very effective fire by the Serbs. Well, you also have to look at this very compartmentalized terrain. You mean the Iranian coast is like a wall of mountains and you can sit on the other side of the plateau and up on those ridgeel lines and you can command everything on the Gulf, which means that you can take aircraft under fire from those locations as well. So, I think it's a very effective weapon. It's a ground support weapon. I no doubt and the pilots are excellent. I know they'll do a good job, but I think it's hazardous and we may be surprised that we lose some aircraft as a result. But I'm most concerned about the men on the ground because once you put them out there if you're halfway between, say, the UAE and uh Iran, how do you get food and water to them? How do you ensure that you can evacuate wounded? How do you prevent people that are severely wounded from dying of wounds? And this is not an easy an easy prospect. Now, you talked about the special operations inside Iran. Iran is enormous. Now, they can probably slip in through the south in some capacity, maybe on the edge of a desert or something. They're still going to be picked up. The the satellite surveillance is going to find them. The question is, can they get in and go to whatever locations they've been directed to go to, confirm anything, and do anything of significance? I'm I'm just not convinced they can. And you're talking about excellent soldiers. That's this is that's not the issue here. It's mission impossible. It's like looking for a a needle in a hay stack. So even if you arrive at a location where they have evidence that there may be radioactive elements there, in other words, enriched uranium plutonium, you how do you get out? How do you find it? How do you pick it up and bring it back with you? I I don't know. But the these things are all dangerous. Again, as long as you have persistent surveillance, and that's what the Iranians have. They can see everything all the time. As long as you've got that, you can strike at any point inside the country or outside of Iran within a thousand miles. In other words, you don't have to be Johnny on the spot to kill anybody or stop something. But then you can also direct your ground forces. They have a million men under arms and they are not all concentrated in one area. They are dispersed so that they can respond rapidly as needed. Uh, I I would not execute the ground operation at this point. It It's not something that I would do, but I think you're going to see something. Maybe it's Car Island, but then again, how do you get there? Do you come from from the other side of the Gulf? Do you come from Kuwait? I I don't know. I have no idea. A a as a as a sort of gallant top military man, are you worried that Donald Trump is putting men and women of the American military in harm's way for a war the stated goals of which are not clear to anybody either in the United States of America or anywhere in the world? Well, whatever anybody says, the reason we are there is to compel Iran to meet Israel's demands. That's why we're there. The Israelis want uh under no circumstances any form of uranium enrichment. They don't care what percentage. They don't want any. Number two, uh they don't want Iran to communicate, cooperate with or talk to any of their partners in the region, whether they're the Houdis or Hezbollah or anybody else. You know, and I think the other thing that they want is that they want the Iranians to dismantle a large portion of their ballistic missiles. Certainly, anything that can reach beyond a couple of hundred miles. Now the Iranians have made it very clear that they were willing to talk about the uranium enrichment and they actually came according to certain sources quite a distance in the direction of what the Israelis wanted on that. Not complete but close. And they absolutely cannot compromise on their ballistic missile arsenal for reasons that we're already seeing. It's very it's very lethal. It's their only means of self-defense plus the drones. and they're not going to stop cooperating with others in the region who cooperate with them. So, we're there to change that. That's really the truth. Now, we thought that that decapitation as an initial phase, in other words, go in and kill the top leadership could precipitate the disintegration of the Iranian state. That this would then provoke another uprising of sorts. And we have grossly exaggerated the size and scope of that unrest that uh we're seeing the president said in his speech 45,000 people killed complete nonsense. We can't go beyond 34 3500 and we know that the reasons for the killings were that the Mossad agents working there and using 40,000 uh Starlink terminals from Mr. Musk were able to coordinate and organize this demonstration. Then they passed out weapons to the crowd and urged people in a crowd to kill policemen. When that began, naturally the police turned back on them and fired in response, but it was not the mass destruction that he's uh presenting. There are people in Iran who would like to see change. Unfortunately, I think we've killed most of them at this point because a lot of our bombings in and around Tyrron have struck neighborhoods where you had the country's intellectual leadership and in many cases what I would call middle and upper middle class Iranians who want a different kind of government, a more secular government. There's no question about that. And we've destroyed those neighborhoods. We may have killed most of those people. The point is none of that worked. And having failed, they then turn to the disintegration of the state. In other words, what can we do that destroys the state? They don't understand the Iranians. Like India, this is another great civilizational state. And by the way, the Iranians will tell you they owe much of their civilization to you and what came from India. And they have not been your enemy. You've had a good border with them for most of your history. So the bottom line is that they're going to survive. That's the problem for Donald Trump. He doesn't understand and I don't think most people in Washington understand Persia Persia will survive. They're not going away. That's the bottom line. And it doesn't matter what you do to them. My great fear is that when we discover this is a waste of time finally and we stop, we will have done so much damage to the global economy, it'll take years to recover. But then there's always a danger that Israel decides they want to use a nuclear weapon. I hope not because that would be the end of Israel as I think many people have stated publicly. I know this man Mr. Gao who is Chinese has made that very clear that they would become a pariah state and would ultimately not survive. I don't know if they'll survive now. I mean they've taken a beating. Large numbers of their citizens have left the country and more will want to go. No doubt. So the bottom line is uh I guess when fertilizer goes up, the price of basic rice and onions and the street goes up. You you're going to see unrest. You're going to see governments fall. So this is not just a military campaign. It's an economic tax. It's an economic tax on every American citizen. It's an economic tax on every Indian citizen. I I I want to come uh to the Indian perspective in just a moment, but you made a really sobering point about Israel. One, you said you're there. When you say you, I mean the American state is in Iran because of Israel. And two, you said you believe that it is no longer alarmist to say that there is a real danger that at some point the Netanyahu Israel will decide to use a nuclear weapon. And I'm going to ask you this very carefully and ask you to elaborate on this. Well, I think that danger is real. They have roughly 300 plus weapons. They can be delivered from submarine. They have modified German submarines which the German government has gifted to them uh so that their torpedoes and missiles can be fitted with nuclear warheads. They also have the ability to deliver it from the air with their air force and they can deliver warheads with strike missiles. They have this thing called the Samson plan which was designed to essentially bring down everyone. If they're going to go under, then they're going to bring down everybody with them. Uh pretty frightening prospect, but there are people that think like that in Israel. Whether or not they're the ones in charge at this point, I don't know. The the issue here is that Israel is embarked on something after seven October that was not about revenge. It was about conquest. It was about the systematic removal of millions of people from their homes, either drive them out or kill them in Gaza. And this sort of thing is underway now in the West Bank. They're now pursuing the same thing in southern Lebanon. This is what they call the Greater Israel Project, which is going to encompass vast territories that are not part of Israel, but are belong to their neighbors. All of the Arabs are finally waking up to this. The Egyptians have wakened up to it. Uh they're they're afraid, I think, because they know the Israelis have made it clear that they could use a nuclear tactical nuclear weapon to destroy the Aswan Dam, and that's something they don't want to put their country through. But I think the whole region is now turning decisively against Israel. Now, I would exclude the uh uh Gulf elites, but those people have sold out to everybody. They're they're a money-m operation that doesn't pay any attention to anything else. Uh so that's one that's another reason why I think they may go away. I would say that Sykes Pico, this agreement that emerged at the end of the First World War that created all of these states, they're not civilizational states. They're they are fictitious artificial constructs. I don't know that they're going to survive at all. The Israelis don't care. I mean, as far as they're concerned, that's fine. They they want to expand their borders. Most of us that had looked at this, and I've been to Israel several times. I like the Israelis that I worked with. I like the Israeli military. I like the officers. This is beyond anything I ever imagined. I mean, I understood their feelings and their hatreds, their antipathy, but this is this is something beyond reason. And that's what worries me the most because that kind of irrational hatred is so deeply ingrained that we cannot exclude the possibility that they would use a nuclear weapon. Has Trump lost this war already? I think so. Uh I mean you know here is Iran. It has 93 million people in it. It's the size half the size of India. It's a natural for fortress surrounded by mountains with some deserts inside of it. Everybody, I'm sure, briefed him on this and told him the geographical realities, but I think uh he discarded any any advice or any opinions that did not match his own. And he feels he owes a great debt, and I think he does to some extent to to the Zionist billionaires who were instrumental in helping him win the last election. And the Israel lobby was instrumental in bringing in support for him that he otherwise wouldn't have gotten. I think he has has made a promise that he would act and do this and he's going to stick with it. I'm not sure he is presidency will survive this for the very reason that you pointed out earlier. Most Americans do not support this and once it becomes clear that we too are paying a price economically in the United States, it's going to be even tougher for him to retain his office. There's also a lot of evidence that suggests the last the last administration was horribly corrupt and apparently so is this one. Uh you know that's a big statement and one would have thought that that fear uh itself that his presidency would not survive this war would make him step back but alas we didn't hear that. Now I know we've got just like a couple of minutes left with you so I'm quickly going to get uh to the Indian perspective. uh Pakistan has positioned itself in the last few days as a as a mediator which you know has amused some in India made some in India cynical when it's taken China into its fold. How did you see that development and do you see a role for India that has historic relations with Iran and deep strategic uh alliances um and partnerships with the United States of America. A couple of points. First of all, however sincere the Pakistanis may be, and I'm willing to ex, you know, entertain that, it doesn't matter the Israelis are not going to take them seriously. I mean, remember, Pakistan has promised to nuke Israel if it uses a nuclear weapon. They said, "Well, we'll we'll respond immediately." The Pakistanis have a very close relationship with the Turks, and the Turks have always known that if they needed a nuclear weapon, they could get it from Pakistan. So on the basis of that alone, I'm not sure that that's the ideal platform to come to any agreement that includes Israel. So that's the first thing. Second thing is it was really the Chinese that offered something and I read through the points that the Chinese offered. They were not unreasonable because their focus was to get to the peace the negotiating table and to stop the killing, stop the war. very similar to what Trump has tried to do, he says he's tried to do with Ukraine. And I think it's unfortunate that it has not it has not precipitated anything. As far as I know, there's been no formal response. I also know that anything that we sent to the Pakistanis that were that was supposed to be transferred to Iran has elicited the same response from Iran over and over and over again. We're not negotiating and I don't think they will because the strategic initiative is in their hands. Yeah. Yeah. Do you see a role for India? Would you would you India has kept quiet and focused on getting our own people our ships out. Do look at Go ahead sir. Just wanted to point out that Hexith in the meanwhile has a tweet out on X called back to the stone age. I just don't understand this way of talking but anyway. Yeah, it's it's horrendous. Uh yeah, I I think uh India should get off the sidelines. Now you have what 9.2 9.3 million of your citizens that have been working in the Persian Gulf region. I don't know if they've been evacuated or not. I'm sure some have left and I'm sure and I'm sure Prime Minister Modi is very concerned about that which is appropriate. He's obviously or or his foreign minister has talked directly. Your foreign minister is exceptionally gifted and brilliant man. By the way, I'm sure he's talked to the Iranians and it's quite clear to me that the Iranians want good relations with you and are going to work to get the tankers through the straight of war moves. Uh, so I think that's all good, but I think uh, Prime Minister Modi should make the effort to contact President Trump directly because Prime Minister Modi has good relations with Israel. If the if he is is in a position to influence things, the Israelis are going to be much more comfortable with whatever agreement he comes up with or he helps to negotiate than they will be with Islamabad. And again, I'm not impinging Islamabad. I'm not saying they are not sincere. What I'm saying is that Prime Minister Modi is much better positioned to do that. Now, I don't know what's actually going on. I don't know if your foreign minister has spoken with Secretary Rubio or with President Trump. I hope he has because India really is a great power. You have a lot at stake in in this war and this war is is coming to your kitchens. It's going to affect your farmers. it's going to affect your your lifestyle, your standard of living. So, I don't think this is the time to wish everybody well and stand on the sidelines. I think roll up your sleeves and get involved with this because if you don't, this this train has left the station and it's the train to hell and we don't all need to ride it. Absolutely. I I know that Trump and PM Modi have spoken uh but I think India has stayed out publicly and I think what I'm hearing you say Colonel is that it's time to actually get involved to bring the principles to some sort of agreement here. Just a last question. I know you've got a heart out in a minute. You know, I spent about 11 days in the Gulf uh trying to get the perspective from there and I was able to go right up to the edge of the straight of Hormuz from the Gulf of Oman. And I met a mariner and he told me and he told me this 5 days ago. He said, "If this war doesn't end in a week, it's going to push the global economy back by at least four to five years. What is your biggest fear right now? And if you could advise Trump, if you met Trump, let's say you just walked into Donald Trump and you had to say one thing to him for the sake of America, for the sake of the world, what would that be? Uh, end the conflict immediately, suspend all further operations, announce publicly that in the interest of humanity and and the the world, uh, the bombing is going to stop. We're going to end the conflict right here and now. We want to sit down and talk with the leadership of Iran and hammer out some mutually acceptable agreement. And if they don't want to speak to us, that's where Prime Minister Modi comes in. Then they can talk to others. But we're stopping. Now, the problem for President Trump, though, is Israel. Israel wants the destruction of Iran and the destruction of the rest of the world. it seems in the process. If it if it serves their purposes, I suspect that's true. Well, Colonel Douglas McGregor, I've got to thank you for never pulling your punches. Uh I know you're so widely followed here in India. I've got like people are just in the audience stream saying thank you for getting him. He was absolutely the best voice to hear right after that extremely disappointing address to the nation by Donald Trump. We thank you for your time. We're going to let you go because you're much in demand, but we hope to reconnect with you again very soon. Uh, I would have hoped in better times, but it looks like this war isn't ending anytime soon. Thank you very much, sir. Take care. Thank you as well. My best to everybody. Thank you. Bye-bye. And we leave our audience now with the key points of Donald Trump's speech where he said, "Finish the job and put a two to three week timeline on this war ending." How he came up with this figure only he knows. Take a look. Sir, please finish the job. Every one of them. and we are going to finish the job and we're going to finish it very fast. We're getting very close. I want to thank our allies shortly. We are going to hit them extremely hard over the next two to three weeks. We're going to bring them back to the stone ages where they belong. It's great to see you here. Thank you for watching our work. If you haven't subscribed yet, don't forget to click the bell icon and subscribe to Mojo Story and support independent robust journalists.

videos.title: "Catástrofe total": Coronel Macgregor critica duramente Trump em reação impulsiva ao discurso sob...
videos.description:
O Coronel Douglas Macgregor (aposentado) é um consultor de risco geopolítico com décadas de experiência em defesa e política externa dos EUA.

Nesta edição do ANI Broadcast com Ishaan Prakash, o Coronel Macgregor apresenta sua primeira reação ao discurso de Trump sobre o Irã, chamando-o de "teatro do absurdo" e alertando para uma catástrofe iminente. Ele afirma que Trump "perdeu o rumo" e que a guerra vai piorar.

#ANIBroadcast #IshaanPrakash #Macgregor #DonaldTrump #Irã #Hormuz #PetróleoBruto #Netanyahu #Israel #Golfo #IdadeDaPedra

Marcadores de tempo

00:00 – Discurso de Trump sobre a guerra com o Irã
05:23 – Ameaça de atingir o Irã “com extrema força”
06:41 – Usinas de dessalinização do Irã
08:03 – Riscos para os países do Golfo
10:41 – Aumento da presença militar dos EUA no Golfo
14:50 – Riscos das operações terrestres no Irã
18:13 – Negociações de paz EUA-Irã
20:30 – Trump e a OTAN
22:29 – O papel do Paquistão nas negociações de paz

videos.title: Guerra do Irã AO VIVO | “RISCO DE GUERRA NUCLEAR É REAL” | Trump intensifica ataque | Coronel Mac...
videos.description:
#Irã #Trump #Israel

Barkha Dutt entra AO VIVO com o renomado analista militar Douglas Macgregor enquanto Donald Trump se dirige à nação, prometendo intensificar os ataques dos EUA contra o Irã nas próximas 2 a 3 semanas para "terminar o trabalho". O anúncio fez com que os preços globais do petróleo disparassem, decepcionando os mercados que esperavam uma redução da escalada na guerra com o Irã.

Macgregor classifica o conflito como "insano" e afirma que o discurso de Trump ficou muito aquém das expectativas. Ele argumenta que o envolvimento dos EUA está intimamente ligado aos objetivos estratégicos de Israel, alertando que o risco de uma guerra regional mais ampla está crescendo, incluindo o perigo real de uma escalada nuclear.

Em uma forte mensagem para a Índia, Macgregor insta Narendra Modi a intervir diplomaticamente e dialogar com Trump, alertando que as consequências econômicas, especialmente o aumento dos preços dos combustíveis, podem em breve afetar as famílias indianas.

A conversa também examina a possibilidade de uma operação terrestre limitada dos EUA no Irã, destacando os sérios riscos que ela representa para as tropas americanas, a estabilidade do Oriente Médio e a economia global.
videos.transcript: Pesquisar transcrição A very very good morning to all of our viewers in India and across the world. It's a little short of 7:30 in the morning here in India and of course uh getting to be about 10:00 at night in the United States of America where the American President Donald Trump has moments ago finished his address to the nation. Now, for those who are anticipating that he would either announce the exit of the United States from a war that has created cataclysmic and catastrophic consequences around the world, well, that exit announcement, the end of the war did not happen. for those who were looking to Trump's address to the nation to indicate whether the next step would be some sort of ground invasion, limited ground operation, commando action. Well, clearly the American president didn't spell that out and perhaps he couldn't have been expected to either. But what he did indicate is that American troops would be moving ahead to quote unquote finish the job. He said that this was because this was the only way to honor American troops who had been placed in harm's way in this war. It is not the news that the markets, the global economy, and much of the world would have wanted to hear. He did set a time frame, however, the same time frame that we've been hearing for his from his administration for the last many days, two to three weeks. He said most goals, most strategic objectives of the United States of America had been achieved and very shortly and he put a number on it two to three weeks the task would be completely finished. He said talks the negotiations that he had earlier claimed were going really well would continue but if there was no deal then Iran was going to be hit very very hard. The bottom line we simply do not know where we stand. All we know is that there is no end yet to this war. In a moment from now, we'll be going live to the United States of America to talk to a top global military analyst, a geopolitical risk analyst, somebody who's also been a former adviser to the US Secretary of Defense. We will be joined by Colonel Douglas McGregor in a couple of minutes. I'm Barcadat, you're with the Mojo story. On the program today on this special live broadcast, we are going to be decoding Trump's address to the nation. As I said, for those who were expecting that he was going to announce the exit or the end of the war, that did not happen. Instead, Trump doubled down and spoke about how it's going to take at least another two to three weeks before America can achieve all of its objectives. He claimed that most of these objectives had nearly been achieved and in two to three weeks they would be absolutely finished. He used the phrase finish the job to describe what the troops were planning to do next. Ahead of the American president's address to the nation, the A10 attack fleet, the Thunderbolt, had already begun movement towards West Asia, raising anticipation and apprehensions of what Trump's planning next. Would it be some sort of ground invasion? Would it be a limited commando action? We simply do not know. In the meanwhile, it continued to be a night of uh war. Uh for its part, Iran has continued to target uh Israel. Tel Aviv came under intense and enormous bombardment. this even as Iran's president, president Khan released a letter directly addressing uh the American people to say that the American people were not the enemy of Iran. We should remind our audience that Trump's approval ratings are at an all-time low, historically low for any American president and this war is extremely unpopular for him at home. And yet that address to the nation did not bring any closure to the war. I want to introduce our newsmaker early this morning night for him in the United States of America. Colonel Douglas McGregor is with us. He's a top global military analyst. He's a geopolitical risk adviser. He's been uh an adviser formerly to the US Secretary of Defense. We appreciate your time uh Colonel. Uh it's good to see you and I just want to get straight to it because I know you're a busy man and we've got limited time with you. disappointed with the president's speech. Yes, I would say disappointment is an understatement. I had really hoped that he would take into account the really terrible impact this war is having on the global economy. You know, again, our friends around the world are being harmed. The Japanese, the Koreans, you, India is being harmed. Uh you do $180 billion dollars in trade every year with the Gulf States. Those Gulf states, I don't think, are going to survive now. They're in bad shape at this point, but I think the next two to three weeks could be devastating. And it's not just the Gulf States. I I think the whole global economy is in trouble. And I was hoping that he would change course. I really was because he's using a sledgehammer to fix a cell phone. This is a 20th century method of operation. You could have seen this happen in Korea, in Vietnam, and we did. Uh, and and I can tell you frankly, these these massive bombing attacks, even though they become more price, more precise over time, they're devastating, but they're not effective. And you know, if this has been so successful, why is it that every time he gives a speech and talks about how great everything is, you know, the Iranians launch tactical and theater ballistic missiles at all of their opponents and drones. I mean there's no evidence that the capability to reach out with this connectivity, thisworked ISR, intelligence, surveillance, reconnaissance in space and in the terrestrial environment that's also with all the strike systems all over Iran. Some people call it a mosaic strategy, but they're distributed. They're all over the country. They can converge and deliver enormous precise firepower very quickly. I don't see any evidence that that capability has been seriously affected. And they have these underground missile cities and there are there's a lot underground that's stored that hasn't been used. So I I'm I'm discouraged to hear him say those things. The other part of this is if we're so successful, what do we need ground troops for? I thought that this brilliant air naval complex was going to win without any ground troops. They weren't necessary. Of course, the initial expectation was that Iran would fall apart. 96 hours. And now we're talking about bombing Iran into the Stone Age. That's insane. Why? What interest could we possibly have in doing that to the Iranian people? And he says that's where they belong. I mean, these are ridiculous statements that no president, certainly none in my lifetime that I can remember, would have ever made ever about anybody. So, I I think it's the wrong approach. I don't think it's going to work. And I think that the use of ground forces is very dangerous because the same precision weapons, the same uh Chinese and Russian provided satellite coverage is going to be very dangerous for them. It's it's going to it's going to put anybody we put on the ground over there uh in a very difficult and dangerous position. So, I don't see how this opens the straight of Hormuse. I I just don't uh I I'll get to the straight of hormones in just a moment, but may I ask you, Colonel, uh listening to his speech and reading between the lines, is ground action or attempted ground action by American troops now inevitable? Because what else are they going to be doing for the next two or three weeks? You know, I I can't say. Uh they obviously have built up a substantial number of them. I think it's going to reach 20,000 very shortly. Uh but the question is how do you get them there? Most of the United States Navy is hundreds of miles away from the Persian Gulf. They discovered that if they were close, they were going to be struck by missiles. They would be at risk. And so, the Navy has moved away. Now, the Houthis have joined the fight. I suspect the Houthis will probably close the entrance to the Red Sea, which means now you're you're dealing with enemies on on two flanks. You're having to fight in multiple directions at the same time. We're We probably have restocked our air defense missiles, our air and missile defense missiles, but I don't know how many and I don't know how many we still have. We we've obviously used thousands of them. I say that because every time an incoming missile is directed at us, we usually fire two missiles back. Uh so I I I don't know how long we can keep up an intensive bombardment. Now, I think we we expended something like 11,000 precisiong guided missiles and munitions in the first several days at the cost of billions and billions of dollars and that didn't work. I suspect we're going to do that again. But at the same time, you pointed out in just when you came in, you talked about the A10. That is a ground support weapon. That's exclusively what it's used for. And it's very good, but it too is vulnerable. And uh I heard the president say that the Iranians have no air defense. He said they have no anti-aircraft ability. That's not true. Uh and that's not true particularly when you're dealing with ground troops because you've got to come in lower to support them effectively. So at any rate, uh I'm I'm very very disappointed and I'm afraid things are going to get a lot worse. Can we talk about the uh the ATM the Thunderbolt fleet for just a moment? uh this fleet was actually moved even before Trump addressed the nation and it led to widespread speculation in American media and across the world as you mentioned that this is a ground support weapon. What is your biggest fear right now that he's going to go in for some crazed uh what he thinks is a limited ground operation either to take the Cara island or Fox News was reporting uh that he has taken a briefing on whether it's possible to have a commando operation to move uranium. It all sounds frankly insane. And I use that word because it's a word you've used for this war. When you looked at the A10 fleet being moved, when you hear about all this news about the sort of ground ops he's considering as a top military man, what do you think? Do you go, "Oh my god, what's going through your head right now?" Well, you're putting uh small numbers of troops. And you know, I know people will say, "Well, 10,000, that's a big number." But you're not going to put 10,000 on one of these islands. So you're putting small numbers of people in a very vulnerable position. If you're light infantry, the most powerful weapon you have in your organization, frankly, is the radio because it allows you to reach aircraft and artillery. Now, I don't think we're going to get much closein artillery support from the fleet because the fleet's afraid of being sunk, which means that you're entirely dependent upon air power. Now, the A10 is lethal because it can orbit above you at what what looks like a leisurely pace. It has excellent optics. It can pick up lots of information and identify targets on the ground. And its cannon 30mm and 105 millimeter cannon are very, very accurate. If they can sit up there and orbit without being shot down, they can have a terrible impact on the enemy and they can help keep the the infantry on the ground alive, but they may not be able to. During the Kosovo air campaign, which I was involved with, we used some A-10s. And the A-10 found that whenever it dropped below 15,000 ft, all the aircraft discovered this, they were immediately taken under very effective fire by the Serbs. Well, you also have to look at this very compartmentalized terrain. You mean the Iranian coast is like a wall of mountains and you can sit on the other side of the plateau and up on those ridgeel lines and you can command everything on the Gulf, which means that you can take aircraft under fire from those locations as well. So, I think it's a very effective weapon. It's a ground support weapon. I no doubt and the pilots are excellent. I know they'll do a good job, but I think it's hazardous and we may be surprised that we lose some aircraft as a result. But I'm most concerned about the men on the ground because once you put them out there if you're halfway between, say, the UAE and uh Iran, how do you get food and water to them? How do you ensure that you can evacuate wounded? How do you prevent people that are severely wounded from dying of wounds? And this is not an easy an easy prospect. Now, you talked about the special operations inside Iran. Iran is enormous. Now, they can probably slip in through the south in some capacity, maybe on the edge of a desert or something. They're still going to be picked up. The the satellite surveillance is going to find them. The question is, can they get in and go to whatever locations they've been directed to go to, confirm anything, and do anything of significance? I'm I'm just not convinced they can. And you're talking about excellent soldiers. That's this is that's not the issue here. It's mission impossible. It's like looking for a a needle in a hay stack. So even if you arrive at a location where they have evidence that there may be radioactive elements there, in other words, enriched uranium plutonium, you how do you get out? How do you find it? How do you pick it up and bring it back with you? I I don't know. But the these things are all dangerous. Again, as long as you have persistent surveillance, and that's what the Iranians have. They can see everything all the time. As long as you've got that, you can strike at any point inside the country or outside of Iran within a thousand miles. In other words, you don't have to be Johnny on the spot to kill anybody or stop something. But then you can also direct your ground forces. They have a million men under arms and they are not all concentrated in one area. They are dispersed so that they can respond rapidly as needed. Uh, I I would not execute the ground operation at this point. It It's not something that I would do, but I think you're going to see something. Maybe it's Car Island, but then again, how do you get there? Do you come from from the other side of the Gulf? Do you come from Kuwait? I I don't know. I have no idea. A a as a as a sort of gallant top military man, are you worried that Donald Trump is putting men and women of the American military in harm's way for a war the stated goals of which are not clear to anybody either in the United States of America or anywhere in the world? Well, whatever anybody says, the reason we are there is to compel Iran to meet Israel's demands. That's why we're there. The Israelis want uh under no circumstances any form of uranium enrichment. They don't care what percentage. They don't want any. Number two, uh they don't want Iran to communicate, cooperate with or talk to any of their partners in the region, whether they're the Houdis or Hezbollah or anybody else. You know, and I think the other thing that they want is that they want the Iranians to dismantle a large portion of their ballistic missiles. Certainly, anything that can reach beyond a couple of hundred miles. Now the Iranians have made it very clear that they were willing to talk about the uranium enrichment and they actually came according to certain sources quite a distance in the direction of what the Israelis wanted on that. Not complete but close. And they absolutely cannot compromise on their ballistic missile arsenal for reasons that we're already seeing. It's very it's very lethal. It's their only means of self-defense plus the drones. and they're not going to stop cooperating with others in the region who cooperate with them. So, we're there to change that. That's really the truth. Now, we thought that that decapitation as an initial phase, in other words, go in and kill the top leadership could precipitate the disintegration of the Iranian state. That this would then provoke another uprising of sorts. And we have grossly exaggerated the size and scope of that unrest that uh we're seeing the president said in his speech 45,000 people killed complete nonsense. We can't go beyond 34 3500 and we know that the reasons for the killings were that the Mossad agents working there and using 40,000 uh Starlink terminals from Mr. Musk were able to coordinate and organize this demonstration. Then they passed out weapons to the crowd and urged people in a crowd to kill policemen. When that began, naturally the police turned back on them and fired in response, but it was not the mass destruction that he's uh presenting. There are people in Iran who would like to see change. Unfortunately, I think we've killed most of them at this point because a lot of our bombings in and around Tyrron have struck neighborhoods where you had the country's intellectual leadership and in many cases what I would call middle and upper middle class Iranians who want a different kind of government, a more secular government. There's no question about that. And we've destroyed those neighborhoods. We may have killed most of those people. The point is none of that worked. And having failed, they then turn to the disintegration of the state. In other words, what can we do that destroys the state? They don't understand the Iranians. Like India, this is another great civilizational state. And by the way, the Iranians will tell you they owe much of their civilization to you and what came from India. And they have not been your enemy. You've had a good border with them for most of your history. So the bottom line is that they're going to survive. That's the problem for Donald Trump. He doesn't understand and I don't think most people in Washington understand Persia Persia will survive. They're not going away. That's the bottom line. And it doesn't matter what you do to them. My great fear is that when we discover this is a waste of time finally and we stop, we will have done so much damage to the global economy, it'll take years to recover. But then there's always a danger that Israel decides they want to use a nuclear weapon. I hope not because that would be the end of Israel as I think many people have stated publicly. I know this man Mr. Gao who is Chinese has made that very clear that they would become a pariah state and would ultimately not survive. I don't know if they'll survive now. I mean they've taken a beating. Large numbers of their citizens have left the country and more will want to go. No doubt. So the bottom line is uh I guess when fertilizer goes up, the price of basic rice and onions and the street goes up. You you're going to see unrest. You're going to see governments fall. So this is not just a military campaign. It's an economic tax. It's an economic tax on every American citizen. It's an economic tax on every Indian citizen. I I I want to come uh to the Indian perspective in just a moment, but you made a really sobering point about Israel. One, you said you're there. When you say you, I mean the American state is in Iran because of Israel. And two, you said you believe that it is no longer alarmist to say that there is a real danger that at some point the Netanyahu Israel will decide to use a nuclear weapon. And I'm going to ask you this very carefully and ask you to elaborate on this. Well, I think that danger is real. They have roughly 300 plus weapons. They can be delivered from submarine. They have modified German submarines which the German government has gifted to them uh so that their torpedoes and missiles can be fitted with nuclear warheads. They also have the ability to deliver it from the air with their air force and they can deliver warheads with strike missiles. They have this thing called the Samson plan which was designed to essentially bring down everyone. If they're going to go under, then they're going to bring down everybody with them. Uh pretty frightening prospect, but there are people that think like that in Israel. Whether or not they're the ones in charge at this point, I don't know. The the issue here is that Israel is embarked on something after seven October that was not about revenge. It was about conquest. It was about the systematic removal of millions of people from their homes, either drive them out or kill them in Gaza. And this sort of thing is underway now in the West Bank. They're now pursuing the same thing in southern Lebanon. This is what they call the Greater Israel Project, which is going to encompass vast territories that are not part of Israel, but are belong to their neighbors. All of the Arabs are finally waking up to this. The Egyptians have wakened up to it. Uh they're they're afraid, I think, because they know the Israelis have made it clear that they could use a nuclear tactical nuclear weapon to destroy the Aswan Dam, and that's something they don't want to put their country through. But I think the whole region is now turning decisively against Israel. Now, I would exclude the uh uh Gulf elites, but those people have sold out to everybody. They're they're a money-m operation that doesn't pay any attention to anything else. Uh so that's one that's another reason why I think they may go away. I would say that Sykes Pico, this agreement that emerged at the end of the First World War that created all of these states, they're not civilizational states. They're they are fictitious artificial constructs. I don't know that they're going to survive at all. The Israelis don't care. I mean, as far as they're concerned, that's fine. They they want to expand their borders. Most of us that had looked at this, and I've been to Israel several times. I like the Israelis that I worked with. I like the Israeli military. I like the officers. This is beyond anything I ever imagined. I mean, I understood their feelings and their hatreds, their antipathy, but this is this is something beyond reason. And that's what worries me the most because that kind of irrational hatred is so deeply ingrained that we cannot exclude the possibility that they would use a nuclear weapon. Has Trump lost this war already? I think so. Uh I mean you know here is Iran. It has 93 million people in it. It's the size half the size of India. It's a natural for fortress surrounded by mountains with some deserts inside of it. Everybody, I'm sure, briefed him on this and told him the geographical realities, but I think uh he discarded any any advice or any opinions that did not match his own. And he feels he owes a great debt, and I think he does to some extent to to the Zionist billionaires who were instrumental in helping him win the last election. And the Israel lobby was instrumental in bringing in support for him that he otherwise wouldn't have gotten. I think he has has made a promise that he would act and do this and he's going to stick with it. I'm not sure he is presidency will survive this for the very reason that you pointed out earlier. Most Americans do not support this and once it becomes clear that we too are paying a price economically in the United States, it's going to be even tougher for him to retain his office. There's also a lot of evidence that suggests the last the last administration was horribly corrupt and apparently so is this one. Uh you know that's a big statement and one would have thought that that fear uh itself that his presidency would not survive this war would make him step back but alas we didn't hear that. Now I know we've got just like a couple of minutes left with you so I'm quickly going to get uh to the Indian perspective. uh Pakistan has positioned itself in the last few days as a as a mediator which you know has amused some in India made some in India cynical when it's taken China into its fold. How did you see that development and do you see a role for India that has historic relations with Iran and deep strategic uh alliances um and partnerships with the United States of America. A couple of points. First of all, however sincere the Pakistanis may be, and I'm willing to ex, you know, entertain that, it doesn't matter the Israelis are not going to take them seriously. I mean, remember, Pakistan has promised to nuke Israel if it uses a nuclear weapon. They said, "Well, we'll we'll respond immediately." The Pakistanis have a very close relationship with the Turks, and the Turks have always known that if they needed a nuclear weapon, they could get it from Pakistan. So on the basis of that alone, I'm not sure that that's the ideal platform to come to any agreement that includes Israel. So that's the first thing. Second thing is it was really the Chinese that offered something and I read through the points that the Chinese offered. They were not unreasonable because their focus was to get to the peace the negotiating table and to stop the killing, stop the war. very similar to what Trump has tried to do, he says he's tried to do with Ukraine. And I think it's unfortunate that it has not it has not precipitated anything. As far as I know, there's been no formal response. I also know that anything that we sent to the Pakistanis that were that was supposed to be transferred to Iran has elicited the same response from Iran over and over and over again. We're not negotiating and I don't think they will because the strategic initiative is in their hands. Yeah. Yeah. Do you see a role for India? Would you would you India has kept quiet and focused on getting our own people our ships out. Do look at Go ahead sir. Just wanted to point out that Hexith in the meanwhile has a tweet out on X called back to the stone age. I just don't understand this way of talking but anyway. Yeah, it's it's horrendous. Uh yeah, I I think uh India should get off the sidelines. Now you have what 9.2 9.3 million of your citizens that have been working in the Persian Gulf region. I don't know if they've been evacuated or not. I'm sure some have left and I'm sure and I'm sure Prime Minister Modi is very concerned about that which is appropriate. He's obviously or or his foreign minister has talked directly. Your foreign minister is exceptionally gifted and brilliant man. By the way, I'm sure he's talked to the Iranians and it's quite clear to me that the Iranians want good relations with you and are going to work to get the tankers through the straight of war moves. Uh, so I think that's all good, but I think uh, Prime Minister Modi should make the effort to contact President Trump directly because Prime Minister Modi has good relations with Israel. If the if he is is in a position to influence things, the Israelis are going to be much more comfortable with whatever agreement he comes up with or he helps to negotiate than they will be with Islamabad. And again, I'm not impinging Islamabad. I'm not saying they are not sincere. What I'm saying is that Prime Minister Modi is much better positioned to do that. Now, I don't know what's actually going on. I don't know if your foreign minister has spoken with Secretary Rubio or with President Trump. I hope he has because India really is a great power. You have a lot at stake in in this war and this war is is coming to your kitchens. It's going to affect your farmers. it's going to affect your your lifestyle, your standard of living. So, I don't think this is the time to wish everybody well and stand on the sidelines. I think roll up your sleeves and get involved with this because if you don't, this this train has left the station and it's the train to hell and we don't all need to ride it. Absolutely. I I know that Trump and PM Modi have spoken uh but I think India has stayed out publicly and I think what I'm hearing you say Colonel is that it's time to actually get involved to bring the principles to some sort of agreement here. Just a last question. I know you've got a heart out in a minute. You know, I spent about 11 days in the Gulf uh trying to get the perspective from there and I was able to go right up to the edge of the straight of Hormuz from the Gulf of Oman. And I met a mariner and he told me and he told me this 5 days ago. He said, "If this war doesn't end in a week, it's going to push the global economy back by at least four to five years. What is your biggest fear right now? And if you could advise Trump, if you met Trump, let's say you just walked into Donald Trump and you had to say one thing to him for the sake of America, for the sake of the world, what would that be? Uh, end the conflict immediately, suspend all further operations, announce publicly that in the interest of humanity and and the the world, uh, the bombing is going to stop. We're going to end the conflict right here and now. We want to sit down and talk with the leadership of Iran and hammer out some mutually acceptable agreement. And if they don't want to speak to us, that's where Prime Minister Modi comes in. Then they can talk to others. But we're stopping. Now, the problem for President Trump, though, is Israel. Israel wants the destruction of Iran and the destruction of the rest of the world. it seems in the process. If it if it serves their purposes, I suspect that's true. Well, Colonel Douglas McGregor, I've got to thank you for never pulling your punches. Uh I know you're so widely followed here in India. I've got like people are just in the audience stream saying thank you for getting him. He was absolutely the best voice to hear right after that extremely disappointing address to the nation by Donald Trump. We thank you for your time. We're going to let you go because you're much in demand, but we hope to reconnect with you again very soon. Uh, I would have hoped in better times, but it looks like this war isn't ending anytime soon. Thank you very much, sir. Take care. Thank you as well. My best to everybody. Thank you. Bye-bye. And we leave our audience now with the key points of Donald Trump's speech where he said, "Finish the job and put a two to three week timeline on this war ending." How he came up with this figure only he knows. Take a look. Sir, please finish the job. Every one of them. and we are going to finish the job and we're going to finish it very fast. We're getting very close. I want to thank our allies shortly. We are going to hit them extremely hard over the next two to three weeks. We're going to bring them back to the stone ages where they belong. It's great to see you here. Thank you for watching our work. If you haven't subscribed yet, don't forget to click the bell icon and subscribe to Mojo Story and support independent robust journalists.

videos.title: "Catástrofe total": Coronel Macgregor critica duramente Trump em reação impulsiva ao discurso sob...
videos.description:
O Coronel Douglas Macgregor (aposentado) é um consultor de risco geopolítico com décadas de experiência em defesa e política externa dos EUA.

Nesta edição do ANI Broadcast com Ishaan Prakash, o Coronel Macgregor apresenta sua primeira reação ao discurso de Trump sobre o Irã, chamando-o de "teatro do absurdo" e alertando para uma catástrofe iminente. Ele afirma que Trump "perdeu o rumo" e que a guerra vai piorar.

#ANIBroadcast #IshaanPrakash #Macgregor #DonaldTrump #Irã #Hormuz #PetróleoBruto #Netanyahu #Israel #Golfo #IdadeDaPedra

Marcadores de tempo

00:00 – Discurso de Trump sobre a guerra com o Irã
05:23 – Ameaça de atingir o Irã “com extrema força”
06:41 – Usinas de dessalinização do Irã
08:03 – Riscos para os países do Golfo
10:41 – Aumento da presença militar dos EUA no Golfo
14:50 – Riscos das operações terrestres no Irã
18:13 – Negociações de paz EUA-Irã
20:30 – Trump e a OTAN
22:29 – O papel do Paquistão nas negociações de paz

videos.title: 1 MINUTE AGO: Russia Steps Into the War — U.S. Navy Faces Unexpected Consequences!
videos.description:
#iran #iranwar #usa
#iran #iranwar #usa #israel  #russia

American warships are reportedly being hit with a precision Iran could not achieve alone. Destroyers, bombers, and aircraft carriers are described as being struck one after another with targeting accuracy that U.S. intelligence officials reportedly called “exquisite.” In this video, we break down claims that Russia is providing Iran with real-time satellite data, priority target lists, and military-grade intelligence aimed at the most sensitive U.S. assets in the region. We also examine reports that major outlets cited U.S. officials with access to classified assessments, why questions about Russia’s role are drawing political attention in Washington, and how China’s reported supply of drone microelectronics adds another layer to the conflict. If true, this would no longer look like a limited regional war, but a broader proxy confrontation involving nuclear powers. Share your thoughts in the comments, like the video, and subscribe for more in-depth geopolitical analysis.
#iran #iranwar #usa #israel  #russia  #news
